food_name: Poha (Flattened Rice Breakfast Dish with Onion, Mustard and Turmeric)
region_of_origin: India (Maharashtra, North India, Tamil Nadu, Kerala)
food_subgroup: Flattened rice (beaten rice flakes) cooked with tempered onion, mustard seeds, and turmeric; served as a breakfast plate (~200g)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for flattened/beaten rice preparations (poha/aval). Closest parent would be a cooked rice-based dish. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish (cooked, mixed with vegetables and spices) — closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a cooked flattened-rice preparation with onion, mustard, and turmeric tempering.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for poha/aval. A00LC (rice-based dish) is used as the closest defensible parent. Exact code not available in public FoodEx2 browser for this preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for poha or flattened rice breakfast dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard breakfast plate of poha is approximately 200g, providing around 280 kcal, 52g carbohydrate, and 9mg iron.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Poha (flattened rice, cooked)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for cooked poha/flattened rice. Values for the prepared dish (with oil, onion, mustard tempering) are adapted from the ICMR-NIN base values for flattened rice with adjustment for cooking additions. Per-100g values reflect the finished cooked dish at approximately 140 kcal/100g. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Rice, white, parboiled, cooked) used as secondary cross-reference for macro validation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Flattened Rice (Beaten Rice / Poha) — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~52 for flattened/beaten rice is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for parboiled and flattened rice preparations. The flattening process (parboiling then roller-pressing) produces a lower GI than regular cooked white rice (GI ~72) due to altered starch structure. GL per 200g serving calculated as: (52 × 52g carbohydrate) / 100 = 27.0. GI classified as low (<55). This is consistent with published literature on parboiled rice preparations.

hypertension.guidance: Poha contains 360mg sodium per standard 200g plate, primarily from added salt during cooking and tempering; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion to one plate to keep sodium within the 1500–2000mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~52 (low-medium), GL ~27 per 200g serving; substantial carbohydrate load of 52g per plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Poha has a meaningfully lower GI than regular cooked white rice (52 vs 72), moderating postprandial glucose response; however, the 52g carbohydrate load per 200g plate produces a glycemic load of ~27, which is high, so T2DM patients should limit to a half-plate serving (~100g) or pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (0.4g/100g), high iron (4.5mg/100g), no trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Poha is cholesterol-free with very low saturated fat (0.8g per 200g serving) and zero trans fat, making it a heart-friendly breakfast grain; the high iron content (9mg per 200g) supports haemoglobin synthesis relevant to anaemia-related cardiac load, and the turmeric tempering provides curcumin with modest anti-inflammatory properties.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high iron (4.5mg/100g = 9mg per 200g serving); turmeric curcumin; vitamin C from lemon
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Poha is particularly beneficial for RA patients with anaemia of chronic disease (ACD) — a common comorbidity — providing 9mg iron per plate (50% of RDI), with vitamin C from lemon juice enhancing non-haem iron absorption; the turmeric tempering adds curcumin with documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Poha (also called aval in South India) is a traditional Indian breakfast dish made from flattened or beaten rice flakes that have been parboiled and roller-pressed into thin, light flakes. To prepare poha, the dry flakes are rinsed briefly under water to soften them, then tossed in a hot pan with a fragrant mustard seed tempering (tadka) that includes curry leaves, green chilli, and finely sliced onion, seasoned with turmeric (haldi) for its characteristic golden-yellow colour, salt, and finished with a squeeze of fresh lemon juice. The texture is soft, slightly chewy, and light — not sticky like cooked rice. The flavour is mildly savoury, aromatic from the mustard and curry leaves, with a gentle tang from lemon. A standard breakfast plate is approximately 200g and provides around 280 kcal, 52g carbohydrate, 5g protein, and 6g fat. The standout nutritional feature is the exceptionally high iron content: 4.5mg per 100g (9mg per 200g plate, approximately 50% of the adult RDI), attributed to iron concentration during the parboiling and flattening manufacturing process. The GI of flattened rice is approximately 52 — significantly lower than regular cooked white rice (GI ~72) — making poha a more glycaemically moderate grain choice. Poha is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and dairy-free. In Singapore, poha is available at North Indian restaurants and Indian canteen stalls, particularly among Gujarati, Maharashtrian, and Tamil communities. It is known as aval (அவல்) in Tamil and Malayalam, and as Nasi Gepeng in Malay. For clinical nutrition, poha with lemon juice is an iron-dense, low-saturated-fat breakfast particularly recommended for patients with iron-deficiency anaemia, rheumatoid arthritis with anaemia of chronic disease, and as a lower-GI rice alternative for T2DM patients when portion-controlled.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — the primary approved source for Indian dishes — with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as secondary cross-reference for macro validation. GI of 52 referenced from University of Sydney GI Database for flattened/beaten rice. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI are complete and internally consistent. Iron value of 4.5mg/100g is well-documented for parboiled flattened rice in ICMR-NIN literature.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked poha (flattened rice with tempering) is not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the finished cooked dish is not reported in the primary source. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as poha is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero for all vegan plant foods. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for this plant-based grain dish with no fortification. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional poha preparation. 7. GI of 52 is classified as low (<55) per University of Sydney GI Database convention. The glycemic load of 27.0 per 200g serving is calculated as (52 × 52g carbohydrate) / 100. 8. The iron value of 4.5mg/100g is notably high for a grain dish and is consistent with ICMR-NIN data for parboiled flattened rice, where the parboiling process concentrates iron. 9. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest defensible parent (rice-based dish); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for poha specifically. 10.
